titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 31 January 1599

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: I receved your letters of the first and ninth of this present very lately, and marvayle you had not heard of your skipper before that time. I pray God all be well with him, and that he have not miscaried, for you had rich adventure in him of the Thesaurus Geographicus,The Thesaurus Geographicus of Abraham Ortelius had been promised to Carleton since Letter 10 and dispatched by special bearer in Letter 12, at Rowland Lytton's expense. It was apparently sent by a skipper — a ship's captain sailing to Ostend — who has now gone silent. Chamberlain's worry is practical: Carleton had a valuable cargo at risk. sent by your cousen Lytton, who is now in towne, and commends him to you. Your brother Carleton is at length come, and the Thesaurus not yet come to you; and for your other private frends here is no chaunge.

The erle of Essex his commission for Ireland is after many difficulties agreed on, though not yet signed. He hath the name of Lieutenant,Lord Lieutenant General of Ireland — the highest military-viceregal designation in the crown's gift, outranking the more usual title of Lord Deputy. Essex had insisted on this superior designation from the outset. His commission was reportedly the most sweeping ever issued for Ireland: beyond the lieutenant-general's title it granted him power to create barons, to return at his own discretion, and to dispose of lands won from the rebels — prerogatives that gave a faction of the council serious pause. [power to create] a baron or two, may returne at pleasure, make and dispose of such landes as shalbe won from the rebells at his goode liking, and many other such points are spoken of, but how truly viderit successus:"Let the outcome be seen" — the outcome will tell. Chamberlain's dry Latinate reserve: the paper powers are impressive; whether they will survive contact with Irish reality is another question. he makes great provision for horses, and many are presented him. They talke likewise of carrienge over two or three hundreth mastivesMastiffs — large war-dogs bred for combat. The plan to deploy them against Irish cattle was a scorched-earth tactic: starving rebel communities by killing their livestock. As Chamberlain mildly observes, the target would be the cattle rather than the people themselves. The scheme was discussed but never carried out. to werry the Irish or rather (as I take yt) theyre cattell.

The preasse of his followers wilbe much abated by reason the Quene countermaundes many, as namely first all her owne servants, and the erle of Rutland and the Lord Gray, Sir Nicolas Parker, Sir Ferdinando Gorge, Sir Edward Yorke, Sir John Barkclay, Sir John Brooke, Sir Charles Blunt, Sir Thomas Egerton,Not Thomas Egerton the Lord Keeper (already in his late fifties and a principal officer of state), but a younger soldier of the same name — possibly a son or near kinsman — who appears as one of Essex's intended colonels here countermanded from the Irish expedition. Sir Thomas Germin, Sir Charles Davers, and divers other that come not now in my way. Some suspect yt is his owne doing because he is not able to geve them all satisfaction, but I am not of that opinion.The Queen's mass countermand of Essex's followers was widely read as a deliberate check on his military patronage. Chamberlain, with characteristic moderation, refuses to attribute the dismissals to Essex himself — who, he implies, would have strained to accommodate so many ambitious clients had the Queen allowed it.

He hath dealt well aforehand with Cap: John DavisCaptain John Davies (sometimes Davis), soldier and military administrator. The surveyorship of the ordnance — the crown officer responsible for equipping artillery and munitions — was a permanent administrative post, not dependent on the Irish expedition's outcome. "A place will stick by him howsoever this journey succeed" is Chamberlain's way of noting that Essex has done Davies a lasting good turn regardless of what happens in Ireland. and put him in possession of survayor of the ordinaunce, which is a place will sticke by him howsoever this jorny succeed. The unkindenes twixt him and others is not yet reconciled,The quarrel between Essex and the Lord Admiral (Charles Howard, Earl of Nottingham) over Sir William Woodhouse's regiment, described in Letter 15, remains unresolved. Chamberlain feared it would poison the Irish venture from the start. which no doubt will much hinder this action that had need of all furtheraunce.

Newes came lately thence that Sir Richard Bingham is dead,Richard Bingham (1528–1599), soldier and President of Connacht, had suppressed Connacht with considerable brutality for over a decade. Sent back to Ireland in late 1598 to shore up the collapsing military situation, he fell ill almost immediately after reaching Dublin. His death removes the most experienced English administrator in Connacht at the worst possible moment. and that the erle of Ormond hath geven the rebells a blow in relieving a fort and slaine 3 or 400 in which incounter Sir Henry PooreSir Henry Power (c. 1562–1621), knighted in 1599; later created first Viscount Valentia (1621). He was an English officer serving in Ireland. A musket-ball lodged in the skull was an extremely dangerous wound; that the ball "remains there" but Power was expected to survive suggests either a fortunate trajectory or wishful thinking. receved a shot in the head which remaines there, but yt is thought without daunger.

Here is a great and curious present going to the great TurkeMehmed III (1566–1603), Ottoman Sultan. The "curious present" was an elaborate self-playing mechanical organ built by Thomas Dallam, the London organ-maker, commissioned by Queen Elizabeth. Dallam sailed to Constantinople in 1599 to install it and left a lively diary of the voyage. The diplomatic scandal Chamberlain anticipates — and specifically among Protestant German states — arose because England's cultivation of the Ottoman Sultan as an ally against Catholic Spain looked to many Protestant neighbours like trafficking with infidels. which no doubt wilbe much talked of, and be very scandalous among other nations specially the Germans. Upon the Duke of Florence his embargo and complaint of our piracies, here is order taken upon paine of death that no prises be taken in the Levant seas.Ferdinand I de' Medici (1549–1609), Grand Duke of Tuscany, had been pressing London about English privateers seizing Florentine and Tuscan vessels in the eastern Mediterranean. The severity of the penalty — death — reflects both the seriousness of the diplomatic complaint and the commercial stakes: the Levant Company's trade to Constantinople, Aleppo, and Alexandria was among the most profitable in the English economy.

We are like enough to fall at some odds with the French kingHenri IV of France (1553–1610), who had converted from Protestantism to Catholicism in 1593 to claim the throne ("Paris is worth a mass"). The trade policy Chamberlain describes was a straightforwardly mercantilist response to France's dependency on imported finished textiles: by banning them, Henri intended to stimulate domestic manufacturing and ship-building in order to capture Iberian trade for French merchants. For England, whose cloth exports underpinned the entire commercial economy, the prospect was alarming. about his trafficke into Spaine, for of late (meaning to set his people on worke and make them industrious) he hath forbidden all manner of wrought silkes to be brought into his dominions, or any worke made of wollen, (which is contrary to our former contracts of entercourse) and withall doth incourage his people all he can to build and buy shippes thereby to get the whole trade of Spaine, wherin yf he be not prevented, he will sone begger both the Hollanders and us, and hereafter we shall have more ado with him and his then ever we had with the Spaniard. Here hath ben much contesting about yt with his ambassador, and that hotly inough, but we have the wolfe by the eares and know not how to hold him nor how to let him go.

The Quenes want of mony is not yet so fully supplied but that they are faine to descend to mean men, and picke yt up here and there as they can get yt, and you must thincke they were neere driven when they found out me as a fit man to lend mony, but I thincke I aunswered them sufficiently, that where nothing is to be had the Quene must loose her right.One of the few moments in the correspondence where Chamberlain makes himself the subject. Pressed by crown collectors for a forced loan, he pleaded poverty — invoking the legal maxim that where no assets exist, the sovereign forfeits the claim — and apparently got away with it. The self-deprecating tone barely conceals considerable satisfaction. There is a sale in hand of 3000li of the Quenes land, which is thought will help well to stop a gap.

Yt is geven out that the Lord ComptonWilliam Compton, later first Earl of Northampton (1568–1630). His marriage to Elizabeth Spencer became one of the celebrated romantic stories of the late Elizabethan period: Sir John Spencer, reportedly furious at the match, was said to have been outmanoeuvred when Lord Compton spirited his daughter out of the house concealed in a basket. Whether or not the basket story is true, Chamberlain's framing — "it is given out" — suggests the engagement was not yet public knowledge in late January 1599. shall marry our Sir John SpensersSir John Spencer (c. 1548–1610), Lord Mayor of London 1594–95 and one of the wealthiest men in England, known as "Rich Spencer." His fortune came from the Levant trade and cloth dealing. The marriage terms Chamberlain gives — £10,000 down plus redemption of £18,000 in mortgaged land — were a substantial settlement that would have cleared most of Compton's encumbrances. daughter of London, on these conditions that he geve him 10000li redy mony with her, and redeeme his land that lieth in morgage for 18000li more.

Sir William Harvies marriage with the old countesse of SouthamptonMary Browne (d. 1607), widow of Henry Wriothesley, second Earl of Southampton, and subsequently of Sir Thomas Heneage. She was the mother of Henry Wriothesley, third Earl — Essex's current protégé. The marriage to Sir William Harvey had been rumoured since Letter 4 (May 1598) and had evidently been contracted in secret; only now, eight months later, does Chamberlain report it "coming to be published." that hath lien smothering so longe, comes now to be published. I send you here certaine odde epitaphes and epigrammes that go under the name of pasquills. And thus with my harty commendations I commit you to God. Touching VlushingVlissingen (Flushing), the cautionary town in Zeeland held by England as security for its Dutch loans. Carleton was serving in the garrison under the Lord Governor, Sir Francis Vere. Any shift in England's grip on the cautionary towns was diplomatically sensitive; Chamberlain's brief "no alteration" was reassurance that the question had not moved in either direction. I heare of no alteration.

closer.dateline: From London this last of January 1599.

postscript.p: To my very goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostend geve these.
